title: Loops
summary: Bash has three loop constructs: for (iterate over a list), while (repeat while a condition is true), and until (repeat until a condition becomes true). All use do...done to wrap the body.
keyPoints: for item in list; do ... done — iterates over space-separated values
for (( i=0; i<10; i++ )); do ... done — C-style numeric loop
while [[ condition ]]; do ... done — runs while condition is true
while true; do ... done — infinite loop, exit with break
until [[ condition ]]; do ... done — runs until condition becomes true
continue skips to the next iteration

exercises.prompt: What keyword starts the body of a while loop in Bash?
exercises.explanation: Both while and for use do...done. Only if/elif/else uses then...fi.

exercises.prompt: Which condition creates an infinite while loop in Bash?
exercises.explanation: 'true' is a built-in command that always exits with code 0 (success), so the while condition never fails.

exercises.prompt: Write a for loop that prints the numbers 1 to 5, one per line.
exercises.hint: Use brace expansion {1..5} or a C-style loop.
exercises.solution:
for i in {1..5}; do
  echo $i
done
